summary: DHH recounts his journey of becoming addicted to daily news consumption around 2010 and his recent decision to break free from it. After the pandemic news cycle ended and seamlessly transitioned into war coverage, he recognized the futility of obsessive news following and cut his consumption by over 90%. He found that the major events still reach him without active seeking, while the reduction freed him from phone addiction and redirected his attention toward books. The core realization is that the payoff isn't productivity gains but simply a calmer mind and a greater sense of time.
key_points: There's a crucial difference between actively following the news and passively being exposed to it — the big events find you regardless
Obsessive news consumption creates an illusion of doing something worthwhile by being 'informed,' but the residual knowledge and wisdom gained is negligible
Breaking the news habit had a cascading effect: less phone use, less scrolling, and more time for books and deeper reading
The pandemic-to-war coverage transition revealed how breathless news cycles are interchangeable and self-perpetuating
The real benefit isn't the productivity substitutes but a fundamentally calmer mind and greater emotional distance from events you can't affect
money_quotes: Following the news often leaves you with this sense that you're doing something worthwhile. You're informed! But are you really?
The difference between following the news and being exposed to the news is huge.
I've essentially given up on social media too, the news was the last big trap of unlimited scrolling that black mirror contained.
What difference did it make to know all the daily infection numbers? Reading every damn long-form piece on what the pandemic was doing, and what we were all doing because of it?
It's a calmer mind. A sense of time slowing down. A greater distance from the woes of the world you can't affect either way.
You really do have to retreat to a cave in the mountains to fully avoid The Slap, Musk buying Twitter, or The War.
When you don't have a never-ending stream of news, commentary, and think pieces to consume, you're all that much more likely to spend time reading actual damn books.
key_insight: Obsessive news consumption creates an illusion of informed engagement while actually delivering little wisdom, and breaking the habit yields not productivity but a fundamentally calmer mind.
